items.q: Why can thermoplastics be recycled but thermosets cannot?
items.correct: Thermoplastics soften when heated and can be remoulded; thermosets have cross-links that lock the shape permanently.
items.misconception: Thermosets are too dangerous to recycle.
items.random_wrong: Thermoplastics are cheaper, so it is worth recycling them.
items.why: Students miss the chemistry. Thermosets undergo a one-way curing reaction creating cross-links; reheating them just degrades them without remelting. Thermoplastics have no cross-links so they remelt freely.

items.q: What is FEA used for in product design?
items.correct: Simulating how a product will respond to forces, heat, or vibration before manufacture.
items.misconception: Final exam analysis of student designs.
items.random_wrong: Counting the elements in a chemical compound.
items.why: Students unfamiliar with the acronym guess at it. FEA = Finite Element Analysis: dividing a part into many small elements and simulating physical behaviour computationally - saves prototyping costs.

items.correct: A drawing showing multiple 2D views (front, side, plan) of an object.
items.misconception: A 3D drawing showing the object at an angle.
items.random_wrong: A drawing in black ink only.
items.why: Students confuse orthographic (multi-view 2D) with isometric (single 3D view). Orthographic projects each face flat onto separate views - used for engineering specification.
Correct answer: Corn starch. “Crude oil” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Plasticiser. “Stabiliser” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: PLA softens at 60 °C. A car interior reaches 70 °C. By how many °C is PLA exceeded?
items.why: The real figure is 10. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
